podcasts.name: Lenny's Podcast
podcasts.subtitle: Product | Career | Growth
podcasts.blurb: 面向产品和创业从业者，聚焦增长策略、职业发展与顶尖实践。

episodes.title: Snapchat CEO: Why distribution has become the most important moat | Evan Spiegel
episodes.description:
Evan Spiegel, the co-founder and CEO of Snap, is one of the very few people in the world who has successfully built and scaled a lasting consumer social product. Snapchat has nearly 1 billion MAUs, and Evan and his team invented some of the most important consumer products and features, including Stories, AR glasses, swipe-based navigation, the camera as the primary UX, and a lot more.
In our in-depth conversation, we discuss:
1. Why distribution is now the biggest challenge for creating a consumer technology business
2. How Snap innovates at scale with a 9-to-12-person design team: no titles, no hierarchy, hundreds of ideas reviewed weekly with the CEO
3. Why a pure software business is no longer a moat, and what actually creates durable competitive advantages today
4. How AI is changing the way designers work and why they’re now shipping code
5. Why every major Snap feature was copied and how that forced the company to work differently
6. Evan’s prediction that humanity’s comfort with AI will be a bigger bottleneck than the technology itself
7. This year’s crucible moment for Snap
episodes.descriptionCN:
Snap 联合创始人兼首席执行官埃文·斯皮格尔 (Evan Spiegel) 是世界上极少数成功打造并扩展持久消费社交产品的人之一。 Snapchat 拥有近 10 亿月活跃用户，Evan 和他的团队发明了一些最重要的消费产品和功能，包括故事、AR 眼镜、基于滑动的导航、作为主要用户体验的相机等等。
在我们的深入对话中，我们讨论：
1. 为什么分销现在是创建消费技术业务的最大挑战
2. Snap 如何通过 9 到 12 人的设计团队进行大规模创新：没有头衔，没有等级���度，每周与首席执行官一起审查数百个想法
3. 为什么纯软件业务不再是护城河，以及当今真正创造持久竞争优势的因素
4. 人工智能如何改变设计师的工作方式以及他们现在交付代码的原因
5. 为什么 Snap 的每一个主要功能都被复制，这如何迫使公司以不同的方式运作
6. 埃文预测人类对人工智能的舒适度将是比技术本身更大的瓶颈
7. 今年 Snap 的严峻时刻

episodes.title: How Anthropic’s product team moves faster than anyone else | Cat Wu (Head of Product, Claude Code)
episodes.description:
Cat Wu is Head of Product for Claude Code and Cowork at Anthropic, building one of the most important AI products of this generation. Before joining Anthropic, Cat spent years as an engineer and briefly worked in VC. Today, she’s interviewing hundreds of product managers who are trying to break into AI—and seeing firsthand what separates those who thrive from those who fall behind.
We discuss:
1. How Anthropic’s shipping cadence went from months to weeks to days
2. The emerging skills PMs need to develop right now
3. Why you need to build products that don’t yet fully work, so you’re ready when the next model closes the gap
4. Cat’s most underrated AI skill: asking the model to introspect on its own mistakes
5. Why Claude’s personality is core to its success
6. Why Anthropic’s mission alignment eliminates the friction that slows most large organizations
7. Why “just do things” is the most important principle for working at AI-native companies
episodes.descriptionCN:
Cat Wu 是 Anthropic 的 Claude Code 和 Cowork 的产品主管，构建了这一代最重要的人工智能产品之一。在加入 Anthropic 之前，Cat 担任了多年工程师，并曾短暂从事过风险投资工作。今天，她正在采访数百名试图进军人工智能领域的产品经理，并亲眼目睹是什么将成功者与落后者区分开来。
我们讨论：
1. Anthropic 的发货节奏如何从几个月缩短到几周甚至几天
2. PM 现在需要发展的新兴技能
3. 为什么您需要构建尚未完全发挥作用的产品，以便在下一个模型缩小差距时做好准备
4. Cat最被低估的AI技能：要求模型反思自己的错误
5. 为什么克劳德的个性是其成功的核心
6. 为什么 Anthropic 的使命一致性消除了阻碍大多数大型组织发展的摩擦
7. 为什么“只做事”是人工智能原生公司最重要的工作原则

episodes.title: Why half of product managers are in trouble | Nikhyl Singhal (Meta, Google)
episodes.description:
Nikhyl Singhal is the founder of The Skip, a community for senior product leaders; a former product exec at Meta, Google, and Credit Karma; and a many-time founder. He’s also one of the most honest, unfiltered voices on what’s actually happening in product management right now.
In our in-depth conversation, we discuss:
1. Why the next two years will be the most chaotic period in product management history
2. Why half of current product managers are at risk, and what separates those who’ll do well
3. Why you need to find your “moments of joy” with AI
4. The “smiling exhaustion” he’s seeing across the product community
5. The psychological barriers that prevent people from reinventing themselves
6. Why your resume’s fancy logos matter less than ever, and what matters now
7. His prediction that companies will shed 30,000 people and rehire 8,000—all AI-first
episodes.descriptionCN:
Nikhyl Singhal 是 The Skip 的创始人，The Skip 是一个高级产品领导者社区； Meta、Google 和 Credit Karma 的前产品主管；以及多次创始人。对于目前产品管理中实际发生的情况，他也是最诚实、最未经过滤的声音之一。
在我们的深入对话中，我们讨论：
1. 为什么未来两年将是产品管理史上最混乱的时期
2. 为什么现任产品经理有一半面临风险，以及那些表现出色的产品经理的区别
3. 为什么你需要用人工智能来寻找你的“快乐时刻”
4. 他在整个产品社区中看到的“微笑的疲惫”
5.阻碍人们重塑自我的心理障碍
6. 为什么你的简历中花哨的标志比以往任何时候都不再重要，而现在什么才是重要的
7. 他预测公司将裁员 30,000 人并重新雇用 8,000 人——所有人工智能优先

episodes.title: Hard truths about building in the AI era | Keith Rabois (Khosla Ventures)
episodes.description:
Keith Rabois was an early executive at PayPal (part of the famous PayPal Mafia), COO at Square, VP of Corporate Development at LinkedIn, and an early investor in Stripe, DoorDash, Airbnb, YouTube, Ramp, and Palantir. Currently he’s managing director at Khosla Ventures. Also, he hasn’t touched a computer since September 2010 (he does everything from an iPad).
In our in-depth conversation, Keith shares:
1. The barrels vs. ammunition hiring framework (and how to spot barrels)
2. Why talking to customers is actively harmful for consumer products
3. How to identify undiscovered talent
4. Why the PM role is dying
5. The three traits of the best-performing companies right now
6. The specific interview question he asks every senior candidate
7. Why CMOs (not engineers) are becoming the #1 consumer of tokens
episodes.descriptionCN:
Keith Rabois 是 PayPal（著名的 PayPal Mafia 的一部分）的早期高管、Square 的首席运营官、LinkedIn 的企业发展副总裁，以及 Stripe、DoorDash、Airbnb、YouTube、Ramp 和 Palantir 的早期投资者。目前，他担任科斯拉风险投资公司 (Khosla Ventures) 的董事总经理。此外，自 2010 年 9 月以来，他就没有碰过电脑（他所有的事情都通过 iPad 进行）。
在我们的深入对话中，基思分享了：
1. 枪管与弹药租赁框架（以及如何发现枪管）
2. 为什么与客户交谈对消费品有害
3. 如何识别未被发现的人才
4. 为什么 PM 角色正在消亡
5. 目前表现最好的公司的三个特征
6. 他向每一位资深候选人提出的具体面试问题
7. 为什么 CMO（而不是工程师）正在成为代币的第一大消费者

episodes.title: Head of Growth (Anthropic): “Claude is growing itself at this point” | Amol Avasare
episodes.description:
Amol Avasare is Head of Growth at Anthropic, which is going through the most unprecedented growth trajectory in history—scaling from $1 billion to over $19 billion in ARR in just 14 months. Previously, Amol worked on the growth teams at Mercury and MasterClass. Before that he was a founder, and he cold emailed his way into the Anthropic role when no job listing existed. Most remarkably, he overcame a traumatic brain injury from a Muay Thai match that meant he couldn't work for nearly a year.
In our in-depth discussion, Amol shares:
1. How Amol landed his role by cold emailing Anthropic’s CPO Mike Krieger
2. How Anthropic is automating growth experiments with Claude (their internal tool called “CASH”)
3. Why the ratio of PMs to engineers might need to flip (more PMs than engineers) as AI makes engineers exponentially more productive
4. Why activation is the single highest-leverage growth problem in AI
5. Why Anthropic indexes 70/30 toward big bets (the opposite of most growth teams)
6. How he uses Cowork to detect team misalignment in Slack
7. How the company’s focus on AI coding created a research flywheel that accelerated their models
episodes.descriptionCN:
Amol Avasare 是 Anthropic 的增长主管，该公司正在经历历史上最前所未有的增长轨迹——ARR 在短短 14 个月内从 10 亿美元扩大到超过 190 亿美元。此前，Amol 曾在 Mercury 和 MasterClass 的增长团队工作。在此之前，他是一名创始人，在没有职位空缺的情况下，他通过冷冰冰的电子邮件进入了人类角色。最引人注目的是，他克服了泰拳比赛造成的脑外伤，这意味着他近一年无法工作。
在我们的深入讨论中，Amol 分享了：
1. Amol 是如何通过向 Anthropic 的 CPO Mike Krieger 发送冷邮件而获得职位的
2. Anthropic 如何与 Claude 一起自动化生长实验（他们的内部工具称为“CASH”）
3. 为什么随着人工智能使工程师的工作效率成倍提高，PM 与工程师的比例可能需要调整（PM 的数量多于工程师）
4. 为什么激活是人工智能中杠杆率最高的单一增长问题
5. 为什么 Anthropic 对大赌注的指数为 70/30（与大多数增长团队相反）
6. 他如何使用 Cowork 来检测 Slack 中的团队失调
7. 该公司对人工智能编码的关注如何创建了一个研究飞轮来加速他们的模型

episodes.title: An AI state of the union: We’ve passed the inflection point, dark factories are coming, and automation timelines | Simon Willison
episodes.description:
Simon Willison is a prolific independent software developer, a blogger, and one of the most visible and trusted voices on the impact AI is having on builders. He co-created Django, the web framework that powers Instagram, Pinterest, and tens of thousands of other websites. He coined the term “prompt injection,” popularized the terms “AI slop” and “agentic engineering,” and has built over 100 open source projects, including Datasette, a data analysis tool used by investigative journalists worldwide. What makes Simon unique is that he’s made the leap from traditional software engineering to AI-native development more fully and visibly than almost anyone—and he’s been documenting everything he learns in real time on his blog, SimonWillison.net.
In our in-depth conversation, Simon shares:
1. Why November 2025 was the inflection point when AI coding agents crossed from “mostly works” to “actually works”
2. How Simon writes 95% of his code from his phone now and why he’s mentally exhausted by 11 a.m.
3. Why mid-career engineers (not juniors) are most at risk right now
4. The three agentic engineering patterns Simon uses daily (red/green TDD, templates, hoarding)
5. The next leap: the “dark factory” pattern where nobody writes or reviews code and AI does its own QA
6. Why prompt injection is an unsolved security problem and the “lethal trifecta” that will likely lead to an AI Challenger disaster
7. Why the pelican riding a bicycle became the unofficial benchmark for AI model quality
episodes.descriptionCN:
Simon Willison 是一位多产的独立软件开发人员、博主，也是人工智能对建筑商影响方面最引人注目、最值得信赖的声音之一。他共同创建了 Django，这是一个为 Instagram、Pinterest 和数以万计的其他网站提供支持的 Web 框架。他创造了“即时注入”一词，推广了“AI slop”和“代理工程”等术语，并构建了 100 多个开源项目，其中包括 Datasette，这是一种全球调查记者使用的数据分析工具。 Simon 的独特之处在于，他比几乎任何人都更全面、更明显地实现了从传统软件工程到 AI 原生开发的飞跃，而且他一直在他的博客 SimonWillison.net 上实���记录他学到的一切。
在我们的深入对话中，西蒙分享了：
1. 为什么 2025 年 11 月是 AI 编码代理从“大部分有效”跨越到“实际有效”的拐点
2. Simon 现在 95% 的代码是如何通过手机编写的，以及为什么他到了上午 11 点就精神疲惫。
3. 为什么处于职业生涯中期的工程师（而不是初级工程师）现在面临的风险最大
4. Simon 日常使用的三种代理工程模式（红/绿 TDD、模板、囤积）
5. 下一个飞跃：“黑暗工厂”模式，没有人编写或审查代码，人工智能自己进行质量检查
6. 为什么即时注入是一个未解决的安全问题以及可能导致 AI Challenger 灾难的“致命三连击”
7. 为什么骑自行车的鹈鹕成为AI模型质量的非官方标杆
